question: Safety stock in inventory management is:
options: Inventory that is damaged and cannot be sold

Extra inventory held above expected demand to protect against demand uncertainty and supply variability — it acts as a buffer so that stockouts don't occur when demand exceeds forecast or supplier deliveries are delayed
Inventory allocated to the most important customers
Inventory held specifically for emergency situations like natural disasters
explanation: Safety stock: the buffer against uncertainty. If demand averages 100 units/day and you order a 5-day supply, you'd receive exactly what you need — but only if demand is perfectly predictable and suppliers always deliver on time. In reality: demand varies (sometimes 120 units/day, sometimes 80) and suppliers sometimes deliver late. Safety stock covers the gap. Formula involves: demand variability, lead time variability, and desired service level. Higher desired service level (fewer stockouts) = more safety stock required. Amazon holds safety stock on high-velocity items but less on slow-moving items where carrying cost exceeds stockout cost.

question: Inventory 'carrying costs' include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Cost of storage space and facilities
Capital cost (the interest rate on money tied up in inventory)
The purchase price of ordering new inventory from suppliers
Obsolescence risk, shrinkage, and insurance costs
explanation: Carrying costs (also called holding costs): (1) Capital cost — money invested in inventory could earn returns elsewhere; at 10% cost of capital, $1M in inventory costs $100K/year to hold; (2) Storage — warehouse space, utilities, handling labor; (3) Obsolescence — technology or fashion products may lose value while in inventory; (4) Shrinkage — theft, damage; (5) Insurance and taxes. The purchase price of ordering new inventory is an ordering/acquisition cost, not a carrying cost. The EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) model balances carrying costs (which rise with larger orders) against ordering costs (which fall with larger orders) to find the optimal order quantity.

question: What does 'ABC inventory classification' help managers prioritize?
options: A = items to add to inventory; B = items to buy; C = items to cancel

A classification system where A items (typically 20% of SKUs but 80% of inventory value) receive tight management controls, frequent counts, and careful reorder planning; B items (30% of SKUs, 15% of value) receive moderate attention; C items (50% of SKUs, 5% of value) receive minimal oversight; the Pareto principle applied to inventory — spend management attention where inventory value is highest
A = most important customers; B = average customers; C = least important customers
An alphabetical ordering of products in a warehouse for ease of location
explanation: ABC analysis = Pareto principle (80/20) applied to inventory. Example: a hospital pharmacy with 3,000 medications; 600 medications (20%) account for 80% of dispensing cost (A items) — these need daily count, strict controls, regular reorder planning; 900 medications (30%) account for 15% of cost (B items) — weekly counts; 1,500 medications (50%) account for 5% (C items) — monthly counts, may auto-reorder. This prioritization prevents managing all inventory with equal intensity — an inefficient use of management time. A items deserve sophisticated demand forecasting; C items can often be managed with simple max-min rules.

question: Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory means:
options: Ordering inventory as fast as possible to never run out

Receiving materials exactly when they are needed in the production process, holding minimal inventory — materials arrive just before they are needed; finished goods are produced just before they are shipped; the goal: reduce inventory to near-zero by synchronizing supply perfectly with demand; pioneered by Toyota; requires: reliable suppliers, short lead times, predictable demand patterns, and strong supplier relationships
Keeping inventory just enough for one day of production
Just-in-Time is only possible for digital products, not physical goods
explanation: JIT (Toyota Production System): the opposite of build-to-stock. JIT goals: eliminate waste from inventory carrying costs, expose quality problems immediately (no buffer to hide defects), and force process improvement (with no safety stock, any supply disruption immediately stops production — creating urgency to fix the problem). Requirements: supplier reliability is critical (a supplier who delivers late creates immediate line stoppage under JIT). Trade-off: JIT is efficient but fragile — the 2011 Japan earthquake and 2020 COVID chip shortage showed how JIT supply chains fail when supply disruptions hit; companies with more inventory buffers were more resilient.

question: A retailer carries 10,000 units of a seasonal product that cost $15 each. At the end of the season, 2,000 units remain unsold. The retailer marks them down from $35 to $18 and sells 1,500; the remaining 500 are liquidated at $5 each. What is the total inventory cost impact of the seasonal overstock?
options: $0 — the retailer eventually sold all inventory

Full-price sell-through: 7,500 units × ($35 − $15) = $150,000 contribution; markdown sales: 1,500 × ($18 − $15) = $4,500 contribution; liquidation: 500 × ($5 − $15) = −$5,000 loss; total contribution = $149,500; vs. if all 10,000 had sold at full price: $200,000; the overstock cost = $200,000 − $149,500 = $50,500 in lost contribution margin; additionally, carrying the overstock (warehousing, capital cost during the season) adds further cost; the inventory management lesson: seasonal demand forecasting error doesn't just create a storage problem — it destroys margin through markdowns and liquidation
The retailer lost money because it bought too much inventory at $15
Markdown selling always recovers the full cost of excess inventory
explanation: Seasonal inventory economics: the cost of overstock is borne in markdowns (selling below planned price) and liquidation (selling below cost). Fashion and seasonal retailers (Zara, H&M, Target) manage this risk through: shorter lead times (less time committed to forecasts before demand is known), smaller initial orders with planned reorders of winners, and markdown cadences that clear inventory before season-end. The $50K lost contribution from forecasting error on this one product × thousands of seasonal SKUs = the 'markdown budget' that retailers manage as a core financial metric. Inventory management in seasonal businesses is fundamentally a demand forecasting problem.

question: Walmart and Procter & Gamble pioneered 'Vendor-Managed Inventory' (VMI) in the 1990s. Under VMI, P&G — not Walmart — decides when and how much inventory to replenish at Walmart's distribution centers. Why did both companies benefit from this arrangement?
options: VMI only benefits the supplier since they control inventory levels

VMI mutual benefits: Walmart: reduced inventory management overhead (P&G's brand managers know demand patterns better than Walmart's buyers); lower stockout rates (P&G can see sales data and replenish before stockouts); reduced bullwhip effect (no Walmart order amplification). P&G: visibility into actual retail demand (end-to-end demand signal, not a distorted order signal); smoother production planning; ability to optimize their own supply chain when they control both ends; the shared data infrastructure (satellite-linked point-of-sale data from Walmart stores to P&G) was itself revolutionary in the early 1990s; both companies reduced costs and improved service levels — the rare true win-win in supply chain
VMI creates advantages only for large suppliers like P&G, not smaller vendors
Walmart gave up competitive control by allowing P&G to manage its inventory
explanation: VMI case study: Walmart-P&G VMI (1987–) is the canonical supply chain collaboration case. P&G gets real-time Walmart POS data → makes replenishment decisions → triggers automatic shipments. Results: P&G's order quantities became more regular (better production planning); Walmart's in-stock rate on P&G products improved from 87% to 99%; both reduced inventory levels. The information sharing was the key innovation — Walmart had data P&G needed (actual sell-through) and P&G had expertise Walmart needed (optimal replenishment logic for complex product lines). VMI has since spread across retail — Home Depot, Target, and most major retailers use some form of VMI for major suppliers.

question: Amazon's inventory position for a particular book: it carries 50 copies in its Seattle warehouse ($12 cost each, selling at $20). Current inventory turns = 4/year (sells ~200/year). Barnes & Noble's bookstore carries 3 copies of the same book locally. Which inventory strategy is more efficient and why?
options: Barnes & Noble's local inventory is more efficient because less money is tied up

Amazon's inventory is more efficient despite carrying more: (1) Long-tail economics: Amazon sells books that no physical bookstore would stock (10,000+ obscure titles that sell 1–5 copies/year); the sum of low-velocity long-tail sales exceeds high-velocity best-sellers; (2) Centralized inventory leverage: 1 Amazon FC serves 50 million customers; Barnes & Noble stores 50,000 copies spread across 600+ stores to serve the same customers (with each store needing minimum stock per title); (3) Fulfillment optionality: when a title sells unexpectedly, Amazon can reorder and fulfill from other FCs; B&N faces stockout with no fast resupply; Amazon's inventory model achieves lower total system inventory by centralizing rather than fragmenting across locations
Physical bookstores always have lower inventory costs than online retailers
Higher inventory turns always indicate superior inventory management regardless of other factors
explanation: The Long Tail (Chris Anderson) + centralized inventory economics: Walmart stocks only the 3,500 books that turn fast enough to justify shelf space; Amazon stocks 23 million+ books. For any individual title, Amazon may carry more units (50 centralized vs. 3 local), but for the system of all titles, Amazon carries far fewer total copies because: 3 copies × 600 B&N stores = 1,800 copies of title X systemwide; Amazon centrally holds 50 copies and achieves same national availability. Total system inventory: Amazon dramatically less. Additionally, Amazon turns rare/niche titles that B&N never stocks, generating revenue from zero additional inventory investment.

question: A manufacturer uses MRP (Material Requirements Planning) to manage inventory. An MRP system shows: finished goods require 100 units/week; each unit requires 3 components A and 2 components B; lead time for Component A = 4 weeks; lead time for Component B = 2 weeks. When should the manufacturer place orders for Week 10 production?
options: Order all components in Week 9, one week before production

MRP backward scheduling from Week 10: Component A (4-week lead time): order must be placed in Week 10 − 4 = Week 6; Component B (2-week lead time): order in Week 10 − 2 = Week 8; quantities: 100 × 3 = 300 units Component A ordered in Week 6; 100 × 2 = 200 units Component B ordered in Week 8; this is the fundamental MRP calculation — translate finished goods demand schedule backward through bill-of-materials and lead times to generate component purchase orders; MRP ensures components arrive exactly when needed without building excessive buffer stock
Order both components simultaneously 4 weeks before production
Order components immediately since lead times create unpredictable delays
explanation: MRP (Material Requirements Planning): the original ERP predecessor that automated this backward scheduling logic for complex multi-level bills of materials. For a car with 30,000 components at different supplier lead times, calculating the order schedule manually is impossible. MRP: input = master production schedule (make 100 cars in Week 30); process = explode the BOM × lead times backward; output = 'order 30,000 engine bolts on Week 24, 100 transmissions on Week 22, 100 brake calipers on Week 25.' The genius: MRP made just-in-time delivery of hundreds of components computationally feasible. Before MRP (1960s computerization), manufacturers held weeks of safety stock on everything because the scheduling was done manually.

question: A grocery retailer has 30% of its SKUs that have not sold a single unit in the last 90 days. The category manager argues these items should be kept for 'assortment completeness.' The operations team wants to eliminate them. Who is correct and what framework resolves the dispute?
options: Assortment completeness always justifies carrying non-selling items

Both have valid concerns that require data to resolve: the operations team is correct that non-selling inventory carries cost (occupies shelf space that could be given to faster-turning items, ties up capital, and increases complexity); the category manager may be correct that some of these items serve a 'strategic assortment' role (presence signals to a key customer segment that the store is a 'complete' source); the resolution framework: (1) Calculate the actual cost of each non-selling SKU (lost contribution from the shelf space × the next best alternative use); (2) Test: remove the items and measure category sales impact (do customers miss them or simply buy a substitute?); (3) Distinguish between items that no one ever buys vs. items bought infrequently but by loyal high-value customers
The operations team is always correct to eliminate non-selling SKUs without analysis
Non-selling items should be kept because assortment drives store choice for all customers
explanation: Assortment optimization: the tension between assortment breadth (customer choice, category destination) and inventory efficiency (turns, cost). Data resolves the dispute: (1) Basket analysis — when someone buys item X, do they also buy items in the non-selling category? If yes, the non-sellers may be driving basket composition; (2) Customer concentration — a specialty cheese sells 1 unit/month but that customer spends $500/month on premium products; that 1 cheese unit is cheap assurance; (3) Space productivity — shelf cost calculation: if the space generates $0/unit × contribution = $0 vs. an alternative item generating $5/unit × 30 turns = $150 contribution, the math is clear. The framework: measure the true cost of the space and the true value of assortment inclusion before deciding.

question: Apple pre-announced the iPhone 7 in September 2016 when iPhone 6S inventory still existed in retail channels. Result: iPhone 6S demand collapsed immediately (customers stopped buying to wait for iPhone 7). This created significant channel inventory write-down costs for retailers. What inventory management principle does Apple's product transition strategy violate?
options: Apple's announcement timing was optimal for managing channel inventory

Channel inventory management: when a manufacturer announces a replacement product, it triggers the 'Osborne Effect' — customers stop buying the old product immediately, leaving retailers holding unsold inventory that must be sold at markdown or returned; Apple's strategy creates channel partner inventory risk; best practice: maintain channel inventory at minimal levels before announcing a new product; alternatively, launch the new product with simultaneous end-of-life of the old product (no announcement lag); or coordinate with channel partners to reduce their orders 60–90 days before announcement; Apple typically has exceptional channel management (thin retail inventory by design), but any announcement timing mismatch creates retailer write-downs that damage channel relationships
Retailers should always hold maximum inventory on popular products
Product transition announcements have no impact on existing product inventory demand
explanation: Osborne Effect and channel inventory timing: Adam Osborne announced Osborne 2 (next-gen computer) while Osborne 1 was still on shelves — customers stopped buying Osborne 1 immediately, the company ran out of cash before Osborne 2 launched, and went bankrupt. Apple faces this challenge at every iPhone generation: channel partners holding 6S inventory when 7 is announced face write-down costs. Apple manages this through: (1) Short channel hold (Apple ships to retailers only 3–4 weeks before announcement to minimize channel inventory); (2) Simultaneous launch and EOL (announce and ship the new model the same day); (3) Trade-in programs that pull old models out of customers' hands. The inventory management lesson: product transitions require coordinating announcements with channel inventory levels.

question: A pharmaceutical company holds 6 months of safety stock for a critical antibiotic ingredient that comes from a single supplier in India. This represents $50M in inventory carrying cost annually. The risk management team says 'reduce inventory; it's too expensive.' What factors should the analysis consider?
options: Always reduce safety stock since carrying costs are always too high

The analysis requires comparing carrying cost vs. supply disruption cost: $50M/year carrying cost is the known cost of the current policy; the alternative policy cost requires estimating: (1) probability of supply disruption in a given year (e.g., 10% annual probability based on supplier history, geopolitical risk, natural disaster frequency in that region); (2) cost of a disruption event — in pharmaceuticals, a critical antibiotic stockout could mean: inability to treat patients ($1M+/day lost revenue), regulatory penalties, hospital formulary removal (permanent revenue loss), and reputational damage; (3) cost of emergency alternative sourcing or expedited procurement; if disruption cost × probability > $50M carrying cost, maintain current policy; the safety stock decision is a risk management problem, not just an inventory optimization problem
Six months of safety stock is always the right policy regardless of cost
Pharmaceutical supply chains should always minimize inventory regardless of risk
explanation: Safety stock as supply risk insurance: the economic framework for this decision is identical to insurance premium vs. expected loss. $50M carrying cost = the annual 'premium' for supply continuity insurance. Expected loss without safety stock = P(disruption) × Cost per disruption. If P(disruption) = 10% and cost of 3-month shortage = $200M (revenue loss + regulatory penalties) = Expected loss = $20M/year. At $20M < $50M, reduce safety stock. If cost = $600M/year, Expected loss = $60M > $50M, maintain or increase safety stock. The calculation also changes after a global pandemic (COVID taught pharma companies that 10% disruption probability was too low for API supply from single-source geographies) — updating risk models after new information is good risk management.

question: The 'bullwhip effect' in supply chains means that small demand fluctuations at the retail level become amplified into large order swings further up the supply chain. What causes this amplification?
options: Suppliers intentionally create larger order swings to maximize their production efficiency

Multiple compounding factors: (1) Demand forecasting — each tier of the supply chain forecasts demand and adds safety stock; a 5% demand increase at retail causes a 10% order increase from retailer (adding safety stock), a 15% order increase from wholesaler (adding more safety), and a 25% production increase from manufacturer; (2) Order batching — companies place periodic batch orders rather than continuous small orders, creating artificial demand spikes; (3) Price variation — retailers stockpile during promotions, causing demand spikes followed by valleys; (4) Shortage gaming — during shortages, buyers over-order knowing suppliers will allocate a percentage of what they requested; the bullwhip effect causes manufacturers to build excess capacity and inventory to serve amplified demand that is actually less volatile at the consumer level
The bullwhip effect refers to quality whipping back and forth between suppliers
The bullwhip effect only occurs in consumer goods industries
explanation: Bullwhip effect (Hau Lee, Stanford): documented in P&G, HP, and many other companies. The fix: information sharing. If the manufacturer can see actual retail point-of-sale data (rather than retailer orders), they can respond to actual demand rather than amplified order signals. Solutions: (1) Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) — supplier sees actual consumer demand; (2) Information sharing (EDI, POS data sharing); (3) Reduce order batching (more frequent smaller orders); (4) Eliminate price promotions (EDLP — Every Day Low Price, Walmart's strategy — removes promotion stockpiling). The COVID toilet paper shortage was a bullwhip effect: consumers bought 10% more → retailers ordered 30% more → distributors ordered 60% more → manufacturers ordered 100% more raw materials, all from the same 10% actual demand increase.

question: Tesla maintains minimal finished-vehicle inventory compared to traditional auto dealers (who typically carry 60–90 days of inventory). Tesla uses direct-to-consumer ordering: customers configure and order online; Tesla delivers in 2–8 weeks. What are the financial and operational implications of this inventory model vs. traditional dealer model?
options: Tesla's model creates inventory risk for customers who wait, with no benefit to Tesla

Tesla's direct model advantages: (1) Zero channel inventory cost — no $5B+ of vehicles sitting in dealer lots deprecating and incurring carrying cost; (2) Demand-responsive production — Tesla builds what customers have ordered (make-to-order), reducing overproduction risk; (3) Price control — no dealer negotiation means Tesla maintains list prices with less pressure for discounts (dealer inventory creates pressure to discount slow-moving models); (4) Customer data — Tesla knows every customer directly (vs. traditional OEMs who know only dealers, not end customers); risks: customer impatience (2–8 week wait vs. dealer same-day), servicing needs require Tesla-owned service network (expensive to build), and inability to accommodate impulse buyers ('I want a car today')
Traditional dealer inventory model is always superior to direct order model
Tesla's inventory model is unique to electric vehicles and cannot apply to traditional automakers
explanation: Inventory model as business model: traditional auto distribution was designed around dealer inventory because manufacturing-to-order in the 20th century required 8–12 weeks, which customers wouldn't tolerate. The solution: build to forecast and push inventory to dealers who 'buffer' demand. Cost: $500–1,000/vehicle carrying cost for the 60–90 day dealer hold. Tesla's innovation: customers who want a Tesla will wait 2–8 weeks because the product is differentiated enough to justify it. This only works for a premium, high-demand product. For commodity vehicles (Honda Civic), the wait would send customers to a competitor who has inventory today. The inventory model is therefore strategic, not just operational — it depends on the strength of the demand and willingness of customers to wait.
